blocks.content: As much as I love a good shoot-out or saloon brawl or any of the other classic TV-western tropes, I like it even more when one of those shows gives a detailed breakdown of how the Old West economy worked. Some of my favorite Bonanza episodes were about the nuts and bolts of the Cartwrights’ cattle business and the kind of money their 2,000-acre spread generated from lumber. I mean this sincerely: That’s some drama.

So I’m all aboard for this next phase of Dutton Ranch, in which Rip and Beth get hired on at the 10-Petal and begin to show that haughty, sloppy Jackson family how it’s done. Given how rapidly this series has been churning through stories, there’s no way of knowing if this new plotline will stick around for the rest of the season, or if we’ll be moving on to something else in a week or two. But for now? Bring it. I’ll take all of the cattle-ranch-competence porn this show wants to give me.

What makes this particular twist work is that it involves both of our main characters. At the start of this week’s episode, as Rip is preparing to swallow his pride and offer his services to Beulah as her new foreman, Beth reminds him that she is, canonically, a badass business lady who could make them rich again in a month by striding into fancy offices in big cities and insulting bigwigs. (As far as I could tell, that’s how she made money on Yellowstone: saying mean things to people in suits.) Rip declines the offer, but as soon as we start seeing brief scenes of Beth at home with her laptop, reading up on the 10-Petal, it’s pretty clear that she’ll make her presence known by the end of the hour. And that’s good. The Rip and Beth Show should feature a lot of both Rip and Beth.

Before Beth steps up, we get the pleasure of watching Rip whip the 10-P into shape. First, he wins over Beulah, using Everett’s glowing recommendation, his own reputation as the former boss of Yellowstone’s massive operation, and a promise to remain “tight-lipped.” (“Other people’s business, it ain’t mine.”) Then he infuriates the former foreman, Chet, by walking into the bunkhouse and getting his team to straighten up immediately and get to work weaning calves. When Chet tries to grumble to one of his colleagues about the “spiderweb” Rip just walked into, the other cowboy shrugs and says, “Well, it was past time to wean them calves,” making Chet even madder. Dogs can tell the difference between an actual alpha and a pretender.

Rip ultimately handles Chet’s insubordination by firing his ass in front of everyone, using the pretext of Chet’s mid-shift scuffle with Austin (Sterlin English), the ranch hand who has been most persistent in asking questions about his missing friend Wes. On his way out the door, Chet cackles that the Jacksons are going to chew Rip up, spit him out, and “forget you ever had a name.” But it certainly doesn’t seem like Beulah’s planning to rip through Rip the way she has with so many other foremen. At the end of his first shift, she invites him up to the house for some 18-hour smoked brisket, then offers to pay him $8,000 a month … which he easily negotiates up to $11,000.

Beulah also seems charmed by Rip’s tale of how he, a rough-hewn nobody, wooed Beth, a magnate’s daughter. That story sets the stage for Beth’s dramatic arrival at the end of the episode, rolling up to Beulah’s house uninvited after Rip leaves. Beth too enchants Beulah, first with her obvious love for Rip (“That man has lost more than he’ll ever admit,” she says), then with her willingness to admit that the Dutton Ranch lost its entire stock to disease, and then with her bold plan to save the Jacksons.

It seems that during her day of furiously Googling “10-Petal,” Beth learned that the ranch was severely hurt by a bad drought, and that ever since, it has been scrambling to avoid having its prime land bought and repurposed — most likely by a solar or wind company, Beth figures. They’ve struck deals with big-box retailers to buy their beef, suffering razor-thin margins. What Beth proposes is that Beulah let her use her connections to make the 10-P’s beef business “recessionproof” by improving the quality of the stock and rebranding it as the steak of choice for foodies.

Beth closes the deal by insisting, “You don’t know me yet, but no one will fight harder for you,” which would seem to be quite a swerve from the clash Dutton Ranch has been setting up since episode one, between the virtuous Beth and Rip and the devious Jacksons. But there is always a further swerve. After Beth leaves, Beulah lets a skeptical Joaquin know that she hasn’t become a total sap. People with secrets are “corruptible,” she says. One of those secrets is the Dutton Ranch’s foot-and-mouth problem, which they literally buried. Who knows what else Beulah and Joaquin could dig up thanks to a few inquiries in Montana?

So yes, there’s still a very clear division between the good guys and the bad guys on this show. You can see it in the scene where Joaquin tries to intimidate Sheriff Wade into finding Wes’s wife, Whitney, reminding him yet again of all the money the Jacksons have given him. You can see it in the way Joaquin waves off Chet’s threats to tell what he knows about Wes’s murder. And you can definitely see it when Rob-Will returns from rehab out of the blue and goes straight to Chet’s door. Whatever Rip and Beth’s plans may be for the 10-Petal — and no matter how much Beulah seems to be onboard, for her own devious reasons — don’t expect the younger Jacksons to play along nicely.

Don’t expect Rip and Beth to be taken by surprise either. Rip has already connected the dots between the dead body he found earlier this season and all the talk about Wes. He finally spills to Beth about disposing of the corpse, saying he didn’t tell her because he wanted to keep the sense of peace around their new home — to which she says, “Peace will have to wait.” Let the battle begin.

• With all the action at the 10-P this week, there’s barely time to talk about the tragic end to Carter’s drunken misadventures with Dwight White. After Dwight gives the young man some hard-won advice — telling him he should just let his parents run his life for a little while and not try to grow up so fast — the cops suddenly pull onto the property and start chasing Dwight down before shooting him dead. Sheriff Wade tells Carter that Dwight was a drunkard, insurance fraudster, and thief and not worth mourning, while also warning that the young man shouldn’t question whether that shooting was clean. (“You never say a word, the sun rises and sets like regular.”) RIP, Dwight. Good-bye, Ray McKinnon.
• Everett continues to show great taste in country music, singing along to the George Jones classic “The Grand Tour” — one of the best tear-in-the-beer ballads of all time.

• Beth has very stringent demands for her services to the 10-Petal. She won’t take a salary, but she wants 20 percent of the profits and a hard out after five years, so that she and Rip can go back to their original plan of living out their lives on their own ranch. She also insists that the Jacksons not slap the Dutton name on any of their products. I strongly doubt that any of these parameters will hold.

• What happens with Azul and Zach? I’m guessing that Rip is going to hire them to work at the 10-Petal, which might cause some problems, given Zach’s notorious past and given that Rob-Will has directed some racist comments toward Azul. I do hope they stick around, because they’re good characters and well played. Beth has a very nice, thematically on-point scene with Zach in this episode, where she asks him what he missed most in prison, and he answers, “The peace and quiet of a lonely night.” To which Beth replies, “Not sure I’m meant for peace.”
